revision.comment: 記事作成／徳島県の神社
revision.text:
{{神社| 名称=徳島縣護國神社| 所在地=徳島県徳島市雑賀町東開21-1| 祭神=徳島県出身の[[英霊]]| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社| 創建=1879年（明治12年）| 例祭=[[11月2日]]|}}
'''徳島縣護國神社'''（とくしまけんごこくじんじゃ）は[[徳島県]][[徳島市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。
== 祭神 == [[戊辰戦争]]から第二次大戦に至る事変・戦争等の国難に準じた徳島県出身の[[英霊]]三万四千三百余柱を祀り、相殿に徳島県出身の殉職自衛官二十余柱を祀る。
== 由緒 == [[1879年]]（[[明治]]12年）9月、[[眉山公園]]に招魂社として創建された。[[1906年]]（明治39年）4月、徳島中央公園の徳島城跡に遷座。[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）4月、徳島縣護國神社と改称する。[[1945年]]（昭和20年）7月、徳島大空襲で社殿等灰燼に帰した。[[1958年]]（昭和33年）再建される。[[1978年]]（昭和53年）には御創社百周年記念として境内整備事業が行われた。
しかし、神社の境内が徳島市からの借地である上、史跡文化財保護地区のため諸問題があり、神社を移転することとなった。[[2000年]]（[[平成]]12年）徳島市雑賀町在住の篤志家・川﨑阿佐惠氏が社地を寄進。本殿・社務所を新築、拝殿・手水舎・慰霊碑等を移設し、[[2003年]]（平成15年）9月に遷座した。
==境内社== ;大國神社 川﨑阿佐惠氏が[[1981年]]（昭和56年）[[出雲大社]]より御神像を拝戴して創建した。
== 年中行事 == *夏期 みたま祭 *[[8月15日]] 平和祈念祭 *[[11月1日]] 合祀祭 *[[11月2日]] 例大祭
== 関連項目 == * [[護国神社]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:とくしまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:徳島県の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:徳島市]]

==境内社== ;大國神社 川﨑阿佐惠氏が[[1981年]]（昭和56年）[[出雲大社]]より御神像を拝戴して創建した。

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:08 (UTC)}} {{神社| 名称= 徳島縣護國神社| 画像= [[ファイル:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine.jpg|300px]]| 所在地= [[徳島県]][[徳島市]][[雑賀町 (徳島市)|雑賀町]]東開21-1| 位置= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| 祭神= 徳島県出身の[[英霊]]| 社格= 内務大臣指定護国神社| 創建= [[1879年]]（[[明治]]12年）| 例祭= [[11月2日]]|}}
'''徳島縣護國神社'''（とくしまけんごこくじんじゃ）は[[徳島県]][[徳島市]][[雑賀町 (徳島市)|雑賀町]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。
== 祭神 == [[戊辰戦争]]から第二次大戦に至る事変・戦争等の国難に準じた徳島県出身の[[英霊]]三万四千三百余柱を祀り、相殿に徳島県出身の殉職自衛官二十余柱を祀る。
== 由緒 == [[1879年]]（[[明治]]12年）9月、[[眉山公園]]に招魂社として創建された。[[1906年]]（明治39年）4月、徳島中央公園の徳島城跡に遷座。[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）4月、徳島縣護國神社と改称する。
[[1945年]]（昭和20年）7月、徳島大空襲で社殿等灰燼に帰した。[[1958年]]（昭和33年）再建される。[[1978年]]（昭和53年）には御創社百周年記念として境内整備事業が行われた。
しかし、神社の境内が徳島市からの借地である上、史跡文化財保護地区のため諸問題があり、神社を移転することとなった。[[2000年]]（[[平成]]12年）徳島市雑賀町在住の篤志家・川﨑阿佐惠氏が社地を寄進。本殿・社務所を新築、拝殿・手水舎・慰霊碑等を移設し、[[2003年]]（平成15年）9月に遷座した。
==境内社== ;大國神社 川﨑阿佐惠氏が[[1981年]]（昭和56年）[[出雲大社]]より御神像を拝戴して創建した。
== 年中行事 == *夏期 みたま祭 *[[8月15日]] 平和祈念祭 *[[11月1日]] 合祀祭 *[[11月2日]] 例大祭
== 関連項目 == * [[護国神社]] *[[徳島県戦没者記念館]]：境内にある資料館。 {{神道 横}}

[[1945年]]（昭和20年）7月、徳島大空襲で社殿等焼失。[[1958年]]（昭和33年）再建される。[[1978年]]（昭和53年）には御創社百周年記念として境内整備事業が行われた。
しかし、神社の境内が徳島市からの借地である上、史跡文化財保護地区のため諸問題があり、神社を移転することとなった。[[2000年]]（[[平成]]12年）徳島市雑賀町在住の篤志家・川﨑阿佐惠氏が社地を寄進。本殿・社務所を新築、拝殿・手水舎・慰霊碑等を移設し、[[2003年]]（平成15年）9月に遷座した。

revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]Created page with '{{draft}} [[{{PAGENAME}}]] is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}}</ref> == Referenc...'

revision.text:
{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tokushimaken_Gokoku_Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tokushimaken_Gokoku_Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] }} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:08 (UTC)}} {{神社 | 名称= 徳島縣護國神社 | 画像= [[ファイル:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|300px]] | 所在地= [[徳島県]][[徳島市]][[雑賀町 (徳島市)|雑賀町]]東開21-1 | 位置= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神= 徳島県出身の戦没者 | 社格= 内務大臣指定護国神社 | 創建= [[1879年]]（[[明治]]12年） | 例祭= [[11月2日]] }}
'''徳島縣護國神社'''（とくしまけんごこくじんじゃ）は[[徳島県]][[徳島市]][[雑賀町 (徳島市)|雑賀町]]に鎮座する[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。
== 祭神 == [[戊辰戦争]]から第二次大戦に至る事変・戦争等の国難に準じた徳島県出身の戦死者三万四千三百余柱を祀り、相殿に徳島県出身の殉職自衛官二十余柱を祀る。
== 由緒 == [[1879年]]（[[明治]]12年）9月、[[眉山公園]]に招魂社として創建された。[[1906年]]（明治39年）4月、徳島中央公園の[[徳島城]]跡に遷座。[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）4月、徳島縣護國神社と改称する。
[[1945年]]（昭和20年）7月、[[徳島大空襲]]で社殿等焼失したが、[[1958年]]（昭和33年）に再建される。[[1978年]]（昭和53年）には御創社百周年記念として境内整備事業が行われた。
しかし、神社の境内が徳島市からの借地である上、史跡文化財保護地区のため諸問題があり、神社を移転することとなった。[[2000年]]（[[平成]]12年）雑賀町在住の篤志家・川﨑阿佐惠が社地を寄進。本殿・社務所を新築、拝殿・手水舎・慰霊碑等を移設し、[[2003年]]（平成15年）9月に遷座した。
==境内社== ;大國神社 徳島縣護國神社の現在地への遷座より前の[[1981年]]（昭和56年）、川﨑阿佐惠が[[出雲大社]]より神像を拝戴し創建した。<ref>[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html 徳島縣護國神社サイトの「大國神社」ページ] 2019年[[5月3日]]閲覧</ref>。
== 年中行事 == *夏期 みたま祭 *[[8月15日]] 平和祈念祭（徳島阿波踊り有名連：天水連の奉納阿波踊りが奉納されるわれる） *[[11月1日]] 合祀祭 *[[11月2日]] 例大祭
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[護国神社]] * [[徳島県戦没者記念館]]：境内にある資料館。
== 外部リンク == *[http://gokoku-jinja.jp 徳島縣護國神社]
{{神道 横}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:とくしまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:徳島市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]]

revision.text:
{{draft|draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tokushimaken_Gokoku_Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Wikipeddia drafts]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:08 (UTC)}} {{神社 | 名称= 徳島縣護國神社 | 画像= [[ファイル:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
拝殿 | 所在地= [[徳島県]][[徳島市]][[雑賀町 (徳島市)|雑賀町]]東開21-1 | 位置= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神= 徳島県出身の戦没者 | 社格= 内務大臣指定[[護国神社]]、[[別表神社]] | 創建= [[1879年]]（[[明治]]12年） | 例祭= [[11月2日]] | 地図={{Location map|Japan Tokushima|label=徳島縣
護國神社|coordinates= {{coord2|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E}}}} }} '''徳島縣護國神社'''（とくしまけんごこくじんじゃ）は[[徳島県]][[徳島市]][[雑賀町 (徳島市)|雑賀町]]に鎮座する[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。 == 祭神 == [[戊辰戦争]]から第二次大戦に至る事変・戦争等の国難に準じた徳島県出身の戦死者三万四千三百余柱を祀り、相殿に徳島県出身の殉職自衛官二十余柱を祀る。 == 由緒 == [[1879年]]（[[明治]]12年）9月、[[眉山公園]]に招魂社として創建された。[[1906年]]（明治39年）4月、徳島中央公園の[[徳島城]]跡に遷座。[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）4月、徳島縣護國神社と改称する。 [[1945年]]（昭和20年）7月、[[徳島大空襲]]で社殿等焼失したが、[[1958年]]（昭和33年）に再建される。[[1978年]]（昭和53年）には御創社百周年記念として境内整備事業が行われた。 しかし、神社の境内が徳島市からの借地である上、史跡文化財保護地区のため諸問題があり、神社を移転することとなった。[[2000年]]（[[平成]]12年）雑賀町在住の篤志家・川﨑阿佐惠が社地を寄進。本殿・社務所を新築、拝殿・手水舎・慰霊碑等を移設し、[[2003年]]（平成15年）9月に遷座した。 ==境内社== ;大國神社 徳島縣護國神社の現在地への遷座より前の[[1981年]]（昭和56年）、川﨑阿佐惠が[[出雲大社]]より神像を拝戴し創建した。[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html 徳島縣護國神社サイトの「大國神社」ページ] 2019年[[5月3日]]閲覧。 == 年中行事 == *夏期　みたま祭 *[[8月15日]]　平和祈念祭（徳島阿波踊り有名連：天水連の奉納阿波踊りが奉納されるわれる） *[[11月1日]]　合祀祭 *[[11月2日]]　例大祭 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} == 関連項目 == * [[護国神社]] * [[徳島県戦没者記念館]]：境内にある資料館。 == 外部リンク == *[http://gokoku-jinja.jp 徳島縣護國神社] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とくしまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:徳島市の神社]] [[Category:護国神社]]

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == Annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (Festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial Ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (Annual Grand Festival) == Notes == {{Note help}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Gokoku Shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == Annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (Festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial Ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (Annual Grand Festival) == Notes == {{Note help}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] |draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]

PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == Annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (Festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial Ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (Annual Grand Festival) == Notes == {{Note help}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Hero shrine]] * [[Martial temple]] and [[Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

revision.comment: Turning most of the links here into interwiki links, because we are currently on the shinto wiki and these off topic links are causing issues with redlink tracking. change back if imported to wikipedi

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]

In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == {{Note help}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]

They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == {{Note help}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == {{Note help}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages with templates in the wrong namespace]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == {{Note help}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}
