A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft|draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tokushimaken_Gokoku_Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == {{Note help}} == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft|draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tokushimaken_Gokoku_Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[Category:dd]][[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|lt=Bizan Kōen}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft|draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tokushimaken_Gokoku_Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[Category:dd]][[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

{{draft|draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tokushimaken_Gokoku_Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[Category:dd]][[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:Unsorted link categories]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}}

In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[Category:dd]][[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} {{draft|draft}}{{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tokushimaken_Gokoku_Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii[[Category:dd]][[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | image = [[File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg|250px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | coordinates = {{Coord|34|2|25.7|N|134|33|43.5|E|region:JP-36_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|Year 1879|ja|1879年}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|ja|11月2日}} }} {{Interlanguage link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Japan]]. It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[World War II]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. '''Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja''' (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} ({{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō}}, {{ill|Tokushima City|ja|徳島市|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|ja|徳島城|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:1879 establishments]] [[Category:Tokushima (city)]] [[Category:Military monuments and memorials]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[en:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]|draft}} In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo Taisha|ja|出雲大社}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == Notes == == Related Items == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] {{Shinto Navbox}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tokushimaken Gokoku Jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tokushima (city)]] == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]
