(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, [[:en:Tokushima (city)|Tokushima City]], [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = [[:en:1879|Year 1879]] (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | festival = [[:en:November 2|November 2]] | location = {{ill|Tokushima|WD=Q207309}} }} {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, [[:en:Tokushima (city)|Tokushima City]], [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]], Japan.. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of [[:en:Tokushima Castle|Tokushima-jō]] in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] * [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|徳島縣護國神社|version=102013767|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{everybodywiki link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] {{Commons category|Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11490214}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima, Tokushima]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, [[:en:Tokushima (city)|Tokushima City]], [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]] | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = [[:en:1879|Year 1879]] (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] | festival = [[:en:November 2|November 2]] | location = {{ill|Tokushima|WD=Q207309}} }} {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] ([[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, [[:en:Tokushima (city)|Tokushima City]], [[:en:Tokushima Prefecture|Tokushima Prefecture]], Japan.. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of [[:en:Tokushima Castle|Tokushima-jō]] in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] * [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|徳島縣護國神社|version=102013767|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{everybodywiki link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] {{Commons category|Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11490214}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima, Tokushima]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]]. They were renamed from [[:en:Shōkonsha|Shokonsha]] in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the [[:en:Potsdam Declaration|Potsdam Declaration]] in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the [[:en:en:List of military occupations|occupation]], Gokoku Shrine was considered a [[:en:Militarism|militarist]] institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the [[:en:Treaty of San Francisco|San Francisco Peace Treaty]] went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its [[:en:Sovereignty|Sovereignty]], the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of [[:en:Tokushima Castle|Tokushima-jō]] in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from [[:en:Izumo-taisha|Izumo Taisha]] and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[:en:Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[:en:National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[:en:Eternal Spring Shrine|Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:The common end of myriad good deeds|The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] * [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == {{reflist|draft}} == External Links == * [http://gokoku-jinja.jp Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja Official Website] |draft}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \\ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|徳島縣護國神社|version=102013767|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{everybodywiki link|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:徳島縣護國神社]] {{Commons category|Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine}} {{wikidata link|Q11490214}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokushima, Tokushima]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|WD=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|WD=Q16154911|lt=occupation}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|WD=Q177266|lt=militarist}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|WD=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|WD=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|en|Tokushima Castle|WD=Q8011246|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|1879|en|1879|WD=Q7783|lt=Year 1879}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|en|November 2|WD=Q2996|lt=November 2}} | location = {{ill|Tokushima|WD=Q207309}} }} {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan.. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|WD=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|WD=Q16154911|lt=occupation}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|WD=Q177266|lt=militarist}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|WD=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|WD=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|en|Tokushima Castle|WD=Q8011246|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult}} * {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=see for the history[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:25, 31 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to {{ill|Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion|en|Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion|WD=Q13429672|lt=Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion}} {{afc comment|1=to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=does not appear to be notable[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 18:28, 1 November 2023 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Tokushimaken Gokoku Shrine Torii.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|34.040472|N|134.562083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|1879|en|1879|WD=Q7783|lt=Year 1879}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|en|November 2|WD=Q2996|lt=November 2}} | location = {{ill|Tokushima|WD=Q207309}} }} {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan.. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|WD=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|WD=Q16154911|lt=occupation}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|WD=Q177266|lt=militarist}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|WD=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|WD=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|en|Tokushima Castle|WD=Q8011246|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult}} * {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|1879|en|1879|WD=Q7783|lt=Year 1879}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|en|November 2|WD=Q2996|lt=November 2}} | location = {{ill|Tokushima|WD=Q207309}} }} {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan.. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|WD=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|WD=Q16154911|lt=occupation}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|WD=Q177266|lt=militarist}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|WD=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|WD=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|en|Tokushima Castle|WD=Q8011246|lt=Tokushima-jō}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult}} * {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|徳島縣護國神社|version=102013767|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| {{Gokoku Shrines}} {{everybodywiki (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City|1=Tokushima (city)}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|1879|en|1879|WD=Q7783|lt=Year 1879|1=1879}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|en|November 2|WD=Q2996|lt=November 2|1=November 2}} | location = {{ill|Tokushima|WD=Q207309|1=Tokushima (city)}} }} {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City|1=Tokushima (city)}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan.. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|WD=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration|1=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|WD=Q16154911|lt=occupation|1=List of military occupations}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|WD=Q177266|lt=militarist|1=Militarism}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|WD=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty|1=Treaty of San Francisco}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|WD=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty|1=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|en|Tokushima Castle|WD=Q8011246|lt=Tokushima-jō|1=Tokushima Castle}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} * {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Q18337501|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References == (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City|1=Tokushima (city)}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|1879|en|1879|WD=Q7783|lt=Year 1879|1=1879}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|en|November 2|WD=Q2996|lt=November 2|1=November 2}} | location = {{ill|Tokushima|WD=Q207309|1=Tokushima (city)}} }} {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City|1=Tokushima (city)}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan.. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|WD=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration|1=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|WD=Q16154911|lt=occupation|1=List of military occupations}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|WD=Q177266|lt=militarist|1=Militarism}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|WD=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty|1=Treaty of San Francisco}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|WD=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty|1=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|en|Tokushima Castle|WD=Q8011246|lt=Tokushima-jō|1=Tokushima Castle}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} * {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Q18337501|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References ==
