(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | caption = Torii |religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja (Tokushima Prefectural Gokoku Shrine) | caption = | map_type = Japan Tokushima | location = {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City|1=Tokushima (city)}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}} | deity = War dead from Tokushima Prefecture | founded = {{ill|1879|en|1879|WD=Q7783|lt=Year 1879|1=1879}} (Meiji 12) | type = Gokoku Jinja designated by the Minister of the Interior, {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} | festival = {{ill|November 2|en|November 2|WD=Q2996|lt=November 2|1=November 2}} | location = {{ill|Tokushima|WD=Q207309|1=Tokushima (city)}} }} {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} (徳島縣護國神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} ({{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}) located in {{ill|Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|ja|雑賀町 (徳島市)|lt=Zagyō-chō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zagyō-chō (Tokushima)|1=Zagyō-chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11659432}}, {{ill|Tokushima (city)|en|Tokushima (city)|WD=Q207309|lt=Tokushima City|1=Tokushima (city)}}, {{ill|Tokushima Prefecture|en|Tokushima Prefecture|WD=Q160734|lt=Tokushima Prefecture|1=Tokushima Prefecture}}, Japan.. It is a [[Beppyo Shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Gokoku Shrines|Gokoku Shrine]], or a shrine dedicated to war dead.{{Efn|Is this [[WP:BLUE]] considering that the shrine is literally named as such?}} Such shrines were made to serve to enshrine the war dead, and they were all considered "branches" of {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|en|Yasukuni Shrine|WD=Q242803|lt=Yasukuni Shrine|1=Yasukuni Shrine}}. They were renamed from {{ill|Shōkonsha|en|Shōkonsha|WD=Q11497810|lt=Shokonsha|1=Shōkonsha}} in 1939.{{Cite journal |last=TAKAYAMA |first=K. PETER |date=1990 |title=Enshrinement and Persistency of Japanese Religion |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/23917081 |journal=Journal of Church and State |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=527–547 |issn=0021-969X}} With the acceptance of the {{ill|Potsdam Declaration|en|Potsdam Declaration|WD=Q163244|lt=Potsdam Declaration|1=Potsdam Declaration}} in August 1945, Japan became the first country to receive the {{ill|en:List of military occupations|en|en:List of military occupations|WD=Q16154911|lt=occupation|1=List of military occupations}}, Gokoku Shrine was considered a {{ill|Militarism|en|Militarism|WD=Q177266|lt=militarist|1=Militarism}} institution and had to be renamed, for example, by removing the word "Gokoku" from its name, in order to ensure its continued existence.{{Efn|Among the designated Gokoku Shrines, {{ill|Aomori Gokoku Shrine|ja|青森県護国神社|1=Aomori Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|ja|和歌山県護国神社|1=Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakayama Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|ja|徳島縣護國神社|1=Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Gokoku Shrine|qq=}}, which was destroyed by fire during the war, did not change their names and kept the name "Gokoku Shrine.}} When the {{ill|Treaty of San Francisco|en|Treaty of San Francisco|WD=Q392541|lt=San Francisco Peace Treaty|1=Treaty of San Francisco}} went into effect in 1952 and Japan regained its {{ill|Sovereignty|en|Sovereignty|WD=Q42008|lt=Sovereignty|1=Sovereignty}}, the majority of the renamed shrines returned to their former names. After {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, some of the designated shrines of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] became [[Beppyo Shrine|Beppyo Shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines over 34,300 war dead from Tokushima Prefecture who perished in national crises, from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} to World War II. It also enshrines over 20 Tokushima-born Self-Defense Forces personnel who died in the line of duty, enshrined together in the same sanctuary. == History == In September 1879 (Meiji 12), the shrine was established as a Shōkonsha (Invitational Spirit Shrine) at {{ill|Bizan Park|ja|眉山公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizan Park|1=Bizan Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582580}}. In April 1906 (Meiji 39), it was relocated to the former site of {{ill|Tokushima Castle|en|Tokushima Castle|WD=Q8011246|lt=Tokushima-jō|1=Tokushima Castle}} in Tokushima Central Park. In April 1939 (Shōwa 14), it was renamed Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja. In July 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the {{ill|Tokushima air raid|ja|徳島大空襲|lt=Tokushima Air Raid|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima air raid|1=Bombing of Tokushima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11489701}}, but rebuilt in 1958 (Shōwa 33). In 1978 (Shōwa 53), on the centennial anniversary of its founding, a project to improve the precincts was carried out. Because the shrine’s precincts were on land borrowed from Tokushima City and located within a protected historical/cultural property zone, various issues arose. As a result, the shrine decided to relocate. In December 2000 (Heisei 12), a benefactor named Kawasaki Asae from Zagyō-chō donated land for the new site. A new main hall and administrative office were constructed, and the haiden, temizuya (purification basin), memorial monuments, and other structures were moved. The shrine was relocated in September 2003 (Heisei 15). == Auxiliary Shrine == ; Ōkuni Jinja Before Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja moved to its current location, in 1981 (Shōwa 56), Kawasaki Asae received a sacred image from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|WD=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}} and founded this shrine.[http://gokoku-jinja.jp/ohkuni.html Tokushima-ken Gokoku Jinja site, "Ōkuni Jinja" page], accessed May 3, 2019 == annual Events == * Summer: Mitama Matsuri (festival of Souls) * August 15: Peace Memorial ceremony (Awa Odori by the famous Tensui-ren troupe from Tokushima’s Awa Odori is dedicated) * November 1: Gōshi-sai (Ritual of Joint Enshrinement) * November 2: Reisai (annual Grand festival) == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} * {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} * {{ill|Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|ja|徳島県戦没者記念館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Prefectural War Dead Memorial Museum|1=Q18337501|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q18337501}} - A war memorial museum located within the shrine’s precincts. == Notes == {{Notelist|draft}} == References ==
